critic_raw:
The most fundamental flaw is the stratified random split applied to time-series IoT sensor data. Manufacturing equipment failure events are not exchangeable — they exhibit temporal autocorrelation (progressive degradation patterns), regime changes (tool wear, seasonal loads), and non-stationary behavior. A random split will scatter pre-failure signal windows into both train and test sets, allowing the model to see the tail of a degradation sequence in training while the evaluation set contains mid-sequence windows from the same failure event. This inflates apparent performance because the model is partially interpolating within observed failure trajectories rather than extrapolating to novel ones. The design justifies the random split by invoking 'cross-sectional signal' — but the entire hypothesis is about temporal patterns, so the justification contradicts the hypothesis.

Second, the label definition is underspecified. 'Significant deviations from normal operational patterns' is a circular definition when the model itself is trained to detect deviations. If anomaly labels are derived from the same sensor signals used as features, there is likely label contamination: the labeled anomalies will be trivially detectable by the CNN because the labeling process already encodes what the model needs to learn. The design should clarify whether labels come from independent maintenance event logs, not from the sensor signal itself.

Third, the evaluation metric is false positive rate reduction — but without specifying the operating point or holding recall fixed. A model that simply predicts 'no anomaly' for everything achieves perfect false positive rate while failing completely on the actual task. The design names 'anomaly detection lead time' as a secondary metric, but lead time only applies to true positives; without reporting it jointly with recall at a fixed threshold, it cannot distinguish early detection from near-zero detection.

Fourth, the CNN architecture is justified by 'spatial hierarchies in time-series data' — this is an imprecise motivation. CNNs process local temporal patches; they are not inherently superior to temporal convolutional networks or LSTMs for degradation modeling. Without an ablation against a simpler temporal baseline (e.g., a windowed feature set with gradient boosting), the claim that CNN structure drives any observed improvement is untestable.

Fifth, the threshold-based baseline tuning is described as 'matched to complexity permitted' — this is vague and operationally important. If the threshold baseline is tuned post-hoc on the same validation data used to select the CNN, the comparison is fair only if the threshold system's tuning degrees of freedom are comparable to the CNN's hyperparameter count, which they almost certainly are not. An over-tuned threshold baseline could actually mask CNN gains; an under-tuned one could inflate them.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split is inappropriate for temporal equipment degradation data: pre-failure signal windows from the same failure event will appear in both train and test, allowing interpolation within degradation trajectories rather than generalization to novel failure events, inflating performance metrics.

Anomaly labels defined as 'significant deviations from normal operational patterns' are potentially circular — if derived from the same sensor signals used as model features, the labels encode exactly what the model needs to learn, eliminating meaningful generalization.

False positive rate as primary metric without fixing recall or specifying an operating threshold is an incomplete evaluation criterion — a null model that never predicts anomalies achieves perfect FPR, making the metric uninformative without accompanying sensitivity.

CNN architectural choice is justified by 'spatial hierarchies' which is imprecise for 1D temporal data; no ablation against simpler temporal baselines (e.g., windowed features + GBT) means the architecture cannot be credited for any observed improvement.

Threshold baseline tuning described as 'matched to complexity permitted' is operationally vague — the number of tuning degrees of freedom for a threshold system is far smaller than a CNN, making the comparison potentially unfair in either direction depending on implementation.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split is inappropriate for temporal equipment degradation data: pre-failure signal windows from the same failure event will appear in both train and test, allowing interpolation within degradation trajectories rather than generalization to novel failure events, inflating performance metrics.

Anomaly labels defined as 'significant deviations from normal operational patterns' are potentially circular — if derived from the same sensor signals used as model features, the labels encode exactly what the model needs to learn, eliminating meaningful generalization.

False positive rate as primary metric without fixing recall or specifying an operating threshold is an incomplete evaluation criterion — a null model that never predicts anomalies achieves perfect FPR, making the metric uninformative without accompanying sensitivity.

CNN architectural choice is justified by 'spatial hierarchies' which is imprecise for 1D temporal data; no ablation against simpler temporal baselines (e.g., windowed features + GBT) means the architecture cannot be credited for any observed improvement.

Threshold baseline tuning described as 'matched to complexity permitted' is operationally vague — the number of tuning degrees of freedom for a threshold system is far smaller than a CNN, making the comparison potentially unfair in either direction depending on implementation.
